title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: In sickle cell anemia, there is a problem with your hemoglobin that makes your red blood cells sickle shaped instead of round.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: This is caused by a defective gene that is passed from both parents to children through genetic material in yourselves, called DNA. DNA is made up of sections called genes. Two of the genes called alfaglobin and beta-globin contain the blueprints for making the hemoglobin

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: In sickle cell anemia, the beta-globin gene has changed or mutated. This mutation leads to the formation of an abnormal hemoglobin protein called hemoglobin S.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Hemoglobin S forms into rigid strands inside your red blood cells, which causes the red blood cell to be sickle shaped.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Sickle cells can block blood flow and can cause pain and tissue damage. And while normal red blood cells last 90 to 120 days, sickle cells only last 10 to 20 Your body may not be able to keep up with how fast the cells are destroyed. As a result, your blood has less red blood cells than normal. This condition is called anemia.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: The symptoms of sickle cell anemia vary from person to person, but may include episodes of pain in your body parts such as your hands and feet, lower back, chest, and abdomen. A yellow tint in the whites of the eyes and skin called jaundice and feeling tired due

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Over time sickle cell anemia can lead to complications such as severe pain and damage to your eyes, heart, lungs, liver, kidneys, and joints.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Damage to your spleen and organ that makes immune cells can increase your risk of infections.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: The risk for sickle cell anemia is highest among people with ancestors from sub-Saharan Africa, Hispanic areas of South America, the Caribbean and Central America, Saudi Arabia, and Mediterranean countries.

title: ANH22254 Understanding Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: To find out more about sickle cell anemia, talk to your healthcare provider.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Cycle cell anemia is an inherited blood disease that affects your red blood cells. In this disease, an abnormal protein molecule called hemoglobin S changes your disc-shaped flexible red blood cells into rigid, sickle-shaped cells.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Cycle cells can block blood flow and can cause pain and tissue damage. And sickle cells don't last as long as normal red blood cells. As a result, your blood has less red blood cells than normal. This condition is called anemia.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Treatment options for sickle cell anemia may include medicines or procedures to help relieve symptoms, or in certain cases a hematopoid-extem cell transplant to potentially cure the disease.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Antibiotics and vaccine shots for diseases can help prevent infections.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Your healthcare provider may advise you to take pain relievers for severe or chronic pain.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: One of the most common medicines for long-term treatment of sickle cell anemia is called hydroxyuria. It helps reduce pain by preventing damage to your red blood cells. It also reduces the risk of long-term complications.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Other prescribed medicines that help prevent complications and improve long-term outcomes include chrysanlysmab, buxelator, and elglutamine.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: If you have an episode of very severe pain, you may have a procedure called a blood transfusion. This replaces the sickle cells with healthy red blood cells.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: For young patients who have, or are at risk for complications from sickle-cell anemia, a hematopoid-extem cell transplant may be advised. It can cure some people who have this disease.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: For the transplant, you may be given chemotherapy drugs, radiation therapy, or both. This will destroy the abnormal blood-forming stem cells in your bone marrow.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: Then, you will receive healthy stem cells from either the bone marrow or blood of a donor. The donor is often a family member whose tissue type closely matches yours. The healthy blood-forming stem cells from the donor will travel through your blood to your bone marrow, where they will make normal red blood cells instead of sickle cells.

title: ANH22256 Treatment for Sickle Cell Anemia
text_en: To find out more about treatments for sickle-cell anemia, talk to your healthcare provider.

file: ANH23268_Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach.mp4
title: ANH23268 Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach
text_en: If you have a severe problem with the aortic valve in your heart, your doctor may recommend a transcatheter aortic valve implantation or tovy. It's also known as transcatheter aortic valve replacement or tover.

file: ANH23268_Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach.mp4
title: ANH23268 Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach
text_en: Your aortic valve is one of the four valves in your heart. It lies between your left ventricle or lower left chamber and the large blood vessel supplying oxygen-rich blood to your body called the aorta.

file: ANH23268_Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach.mp4
title: ANH23268 Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach
text_en: When your left ventricle contracts during each heartbeat, it pushes blood through three flexible cup-like leaflets that make up your aortic valve.

file: ANH23268_Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach.mp4
title: ANH23268 Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach
text_en: When your left ventricle relaxes, your aortic valve closes to prevent blood from flowing back into your left ventricle.

file: ANH23268_Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach.mp4
title: ANH23268 Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach
text_en: As you get older, your aortic valve may develop deposits of calcium that cause your leaflets to get thicker and less flexible. In this condition, your leaflets may not open fully. This condition, called calcific aortic valve stenosis, creates a narrowed opening that reduces the amount of blood flowing into your aorta and out to your body.

file: ANH23268_Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach.mp4
title: ANH23268 Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach
text_en: As a result, the wall of your left ventricle thickens because it has to work harder to pump blood through the narrowed valve opening.

file: ANH23268_Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach.mp4
title: ANH23268 Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach
text_en: At the beginning of your procedure, your doctor will make a small incision in your groin. After creating an opening in a blood vessel, called the femoral artery, your doctor will insert a flexible tube called an introducer sheath.

file: ANH23268_Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach.mp4
title: ANH23268 Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation (TAVI) - Transfemoral Approach
text_en: Through the sheath, your doctor will insert a flexible guide wire into your femoral artery. The guide wire will be passed through your femoral artery all the way up to your aorta. Then the guide wire will be pushed through the opening in your aortic valve and into your left ventricle.
